summary: Jeff Atwood shares his discovery of the Atari Archives, which contains high-resolution scans of classic computing books including The Best of Creative Computing volumes from the 1970s. He reflects on Creative Computing magazine's role as the original DIY computer enthusiast publication, full of BASIC game listings and counterculture aesthetics. Atwood connects this history to his own programming origins, recalling countless hours typing game listings into early home computers. The post celebrates computing's countercultural roots while acknowledging that this formative experience permanently cured him of any desire to type code from printed pages.
key_points: Creative Computing was the original DIY computer enthusiast magazine, representing computing as an alternative culture in the 1970s
The Atari Archives preserves high-resolution scans of classic computing books and magazines that shaped early programming culture
Typing BASIC game listings from books into home computers was a formative experience that helped create a generation of programmers
David Ahl, Creative Computing's editor, was also the original author of Minesweeper in 1973
The early computing magazine culture presaged modern blog culture in its DIY, enthusiast-driven approach
money_quotes: In the 70s, computing was almost an alternative culture.
I spent god knows how many hours typing game listings from these books into the Apple //c, Coleco Adam, and TI 99/4a.
It's probably part of the reason why I'm a programmer today.
It's also the reason why I'll never type code in from a printed page ever, ever again. Ever!
His comments on what Creative Computing would look like today remind me a lot of the current blog culture!
key_insight: The tedious act of typing in BASIC game listings from magazines was a formative programming experience for a generation, even as it permanently cured them of wanting to repeat the process.
